Bit of a disclaimer here - I've written about Penpot so many times at this point that including it in another list almost feels redundant, but leaving it out of an open-source Figma alts list wouldn't be right. For anyone who hasn't met it yet, Penpot is an open-source design and prototyping tool built for UI/UX work, runs in the browser, fully self-hostable, and uses SVG as its native format.

The reason it's the one I keep ending up back in is almost embarrassingly mundane - it's a near-mirror of Figma in terms of where everything sits on screen. The muscle memory transfers, which matters more for tool retention than people give it credit for. Because it's built on open web standards, designs are inherently portable too. Devs can pull measurements and code straight from the inspect panel without exporting anything.

Where Figma still pulls ahead is on big-file performance (Penpot's improving but isn't quite as snappy), FigJam-style whiteboarding (you have to download a separate kit in Penpot), Smart Animate, and the plugin ecosystem. None of which I find myself missing that much anyway, but it might matter to your workflow.

It's not that Penpot does anything Figma can't, it's that it does enough of what Figma does well enough that I forget I'm not in Figma.

Penpot
See at Penpot

Plasmic is the odd one out

It's more of a visual builder than a design tool

Plasmic is the odd one out in this list because it isn't really a Figma equivalent at all - it's a visual builder for the web that outputs production-ready React code rather than designs you'd hand off to someone else to rebuild. It's open-source, self-hostable, and there's a Figma-to-code plugin too, so you can paste Figma layers into Plasmic Studio and they come out as actual React components.

I didn't know about this tool a week ago so this is more "what I noticed" than "what I've mastered." The visual editor itself is comfortable and familiar; if you've ever opened Figma you'll know your way around within five minutes. There's a proper component library with text, layout primitives, form fields, plus a Section Templates pane for whole pre-built page sections. So plenty of design depth even before you get to the inspector controls for image handling, transitions, and spacing.

The thing it has over Figma is what happens after - Figma stops at the design, Plasmic carries it into something hostable, with a built-in CMS and data source integrations. But if you're never going to publish anything yourself, Plasmic is probably overkill for what you actually need.

Plasmic
See at Plasmic

A week later and the rotation settled itself

Penpot stays the keeper, mostly because the muscle memory factor is hard to beat when you're already familiar with it. OpenPencil is the one I'll be keeping a much closer eye on though - if the prototyping piece lands and the rendering tightens up, it's the most exciting Figma-shaped thing I've come across in a while. Quant-UX and Plasmic both stay in the rotation but for specific jobs rather than general design work.

item.title: You are using 5GHz wrong if you're blindly cramming it with all your devices
item.itunes:title: You are using 5GHz wrong if you're blindly cramming it with all your devices
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
While the share of Wi-Fi 6E and Wi-Fi 7 routers is growing rapidly in many countries, a large chunk of the global population still uses Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 5 routers (or older models in developing countries). Consequently, the predominant wireless bands in most home networks are still 2.4GHz and 5GHz. Most people tend to use the faster 5GHz band for every device that supports it, without thinking much about the potential downsides. The 5GHz band carries greater bandwidth, but it loses signal strength faster with distance as compared to the 2.4GHz band. It also doesn't do well against walls and other obstructions. Even if you think your home network is fine with everything residing on the 5GHz band, you'll be surprised by how much better it can be. You simply need to intelligently offload some devices to the slower 2.4GHz band to improve the Wi-Fi performance across the board.

5 router settings most people never touch that could fix their Wi-Fi problems overnight

Thirty minutes of deep menu diving could set your network straight for months

2
By  Samarveer Singh

5GHz is faster, but not always the best option

The slower 2.4GHz band can sometimes perform better

We've always been told that the 5GHz band is faster than the 2.4GHz one, and should be preferred for high-priority devices. That is indeed true, since the higher frequency and broader spectrum size allow greater throughput and fewer overlapping channels within the spectrum. Anything receiving a strong 5GHz signal will be able to receive more data packets per second, and more devices can simultaneously connect to the router without interfering with each other. That's the upside, but the downside is that the higher frequency waves cannot penetrate obstacles as effectively as the 2.4GHz signal can. Plus, the 5GHz signal loses its strength as the distance from the router increases. Only devices that are closer to it and aren't obstructed by walls or other objects can enjoy the faster Wi-Fi speeds of the 5GHz band.

Your iPad in the furthest room from the router or your laptop on the floor above won't benefit from jumping on the "faster" band if the signal can barely reach them. It also depends on the quality of the Wi-Fi receiver inside your device. While your modern smartphone and laptop may be able to latch onto the faint 5GHz signal from further away, your older smart TV could struggle to do so. These devices will arguably perform better on the 2.4GHz band despite it being technically slower. The better coverage will compensate for the lower throughput, providing a more consistent connection.

2.4GHz wireless isn't great but here's why it's still useful today

2.4GHz Wi-FI isn't as bad as you might think

By  Rich Edmonds

You're creating unnecessary congestion by overloading the 5GHz band

Not everything needs the faster band

Although the 5GHz band is less prone to interference, you can still technically create network congestion by connecting dozens of devices to it. If every device on your network is competing for the same bandwidth, then you're bound to experience higher latency and perceived slowdowns. Your devices might still show excellent signal strength, but the airtime issues that outdated devices can create for their modern counterparts can realistically worsen your experience. This is why it's counterproductive to force every single device onto the 5GHz band. You're creating room for congestion without gaining anything in return. Your smartphones, laptops, and tablets are being made to wait in line due to the sluggish devices using older standards and technologies. And the latter aren't benefiting from the 5GHz band anyway.

If your 5GHz band is still being used by smart home devices, at least the ones that support the faster band, then it's time to move them to the 2.4GHz band. This will not only prevent slowdowns for high-priority devices on the 5GHz band, but also improve the performance for home automation devices that need better coverage rather than faster speeds.

You're wrecking your Wi-Fi speeds by ignoring the one setting nobody talks about

Channel width can seriously impact your Wi-Fi performance

By  Tanveer Singh

Decide between 2.4GHz and 5GHz for every device

Optimize your network

The best way to optimize your dual-band connection is to decide on a per-device basis. Does the device in question actually need the extra bandwidth of the 5GHz band, and is it in the appropriate range to catch the signal? If yes, then it makes sense to upgrade it to the faster band. If not, then the 2.4GHz band is the logical destination. Even if your router supports band steering, i.e, switching your device from 2.4GHz to 5GHz on the fly, it is often better to separate the 2.4GHz and 5GHz bands into different SSIDs. Assigning every device to the appropriate network is usually preferred to avoid the "sticky connect" issue, where your router keeps your device connected to the 2.4GHz band even when 5GHz is within range.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter Wi-Fi choices

Get deeper Wi-Fi insight - subscribe to the newsletter for practical, step-by-step fixes and tested networking tips that focus on optimizing home connections, troubleshooting issues, and making smarter hardware choices.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If your devices support Wi-Fi 6E or Wi-Fi 7, it may be worth it to upgrade your router, even if your internet plan remains the same. Wi-Fi 6E and Wi-Fi 7 routers bring a lot more to the table than just higher maximum speeds. The additional 6GHz band with Wi-Fi 6E and MLO with Wi-Fi 7 provide real-world benefits. They improve signal coverage and latency, manage multiple devices better, and make your connection feel more responsive overall.

TP-Link Dual-Band BE3600 Wi-Fi 7 Router
$85 $120 Save $35
$85 at Amazon
Wi-Fi 7's MLO doesn't make your connection faster, but it makes it dramatically more reliable

Okay, it might make it faster as well

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Don't abuse the 5GHz band simply because it's technically faster

The 5GHz band offers greater speeds compared to the 2.4GHz band, but you still need to decide whether the device in question will actually benefit from making the jump. If adding more and more devices to the 5GHz band makes things worse for your high-priority devices, then keeping your slower clients on the "slower" band is the right call. You'll optimize your network this way, and avoid needless congestion and signal coverage issues.

item.title: OneXPlayer's new 3-in-1 handheld has detachable controllers, liquid cooling, and a 144Hz OLED screen
item.itunes:title: OneXPlayer's new 3-in-1 handheld has detachable controllers, liquid cooling, and a 144Hz OLED screen
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • OneXPlayer 3 features Intel Arc G3 Extreme, 8.8" 144Hz OLED, liquid cooling, Hall-Effect sticks, 85Wh battery.
  • Three modes: handheld with controllers, tablet when detached, attach keyboard for laptop use.
  • X2 Mini Pro swaps to AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 with Frost Bay liquid cooling; smaller, crowdfunding mid-June.

Handheld PC gaming has come a long way since the Steam Deck. As Valve's crowning glory undergoes a hefty price hike, we're seeing companies reveal their own take on the handheld scene, including Intel's big move versus AMD.

Now, just ahead of Computex, OneXPlayer is showing off its new 3-in-1 handhelds that come with liquid cooling and a sleek 144Hz OLED screen, and you can help fund it soon.

The OneXPlayer 3 uses Intel's new Intel Arc G3 Extreme graphics

You can even use the handheld as a laptop

Credit: OneXPlayer

As reported by Video Cardz, OneXPlayer has revealed two handhelds this year. The first is the OneXPlayer 3, and it comes with Intel's newest chip in the handheld front, the Intel Arc G3 Extreme. It also comes with an 8.8-inch 144Hz OLED display, an 85Wh battery, and Hall Effect joysticks

The handheld comes with three different forms depending on how you want to use it. With the controllers attached, it plays just like your regular handheld. Pop the controllers off, and now you can play it like a tablet. Finally, you can attach a keyboard to it, and now you have a little laptop you can take anywhere.

The OneXPlayer 3 should appear on the crowdfunding site Indiegogo soon.

Intel's Arc G3-Series chips are here to power the next generation of handheld gaming PCs

The chip giant aims to shake up the handheld gaming space with its new handheld-focused chip.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

You can now register your interest in the OneXPlayer X2 Mini Pro

In case you're after something a little smaller

If the OneXPlayer 3 is a little too mighty for your liking, there's also the OneXPlayer X2 Mini Pro. It should arrive on the crowdfunding site Indiegogo sometime in mid-June 2026.

Subscribe to our newsletter for handheld PC insights

Interested in handheld PC gaming? Subscribe to our newsletter for focused coverage, in-depth looks at devices like the OneXPlayer X2 Mini Pro, hardware breakdowns, comparisons, and thoughtful analysis across the handheld scene.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Under the hood, we're looking at similar specs to the OneXPlayer 3, albeit with the Intel chip swapped out for an AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 CPU. It also has a Frost Bay liquid cooling system to help keep everything at a good temperature while you game. If you want to register your interest in this handheld, head over to the OneXPlayer X2 Mini Pro Indiegogo page and follow it for notifications when it goes live.

Acer's Predator Atlas 8 is Intel's best shot yet at breaking AMD's hold on gaming handhelds

It has a brand new Intel chip to get the job done.

By  Simon Batt

item.title: Why I bought a 16TB HDD instead of an 8TB SSD
item.itunes:title: Why I bought a 16TB HDD instead of an 8TB SSD
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When shopping for new storage drives, you can choose between a hard disk drive (HDD) and a solid-state drive (SSD). Both have advantages and drawbacks for storing data inside PCs, servers, network-attached storage (NAS), and other hardware. I needed to expand (and replace) my storage pool in my primary storage device, and I ended up opting for a few 16TB HDDs rather than moving into 2025 with an all-SSD array.

The SSD is the better option ... mostly

Faster speeds are met by higher prices

An SSD is my go-to recommendation for anyone looking for a boot drive or somewhere to stash local files on a desktop or laptop PC. It's also what I would typically use inside a server to run Docker containers, virtual machines (VMs), and build wacky systems for doing countless tasks, but when it comes to long-term storage, nothing beats the hard drive. Like many of my esteemed colleagues, I opt for mechanical drives to store lots of data.

But what if you're not planning to save too much data? An SSD (or a few) can make more sense. You'll see more hardware launches from NAS brands such as Asustor and Synology that focus on SSD storage, and that's because they can last longer when treated well compared to mechanical drives. An SSD has no moving parts and therefore only really has wear on the internal components through writing, due to how the technology works. The less you write to the drive, the longer it should last.

Other factors can affect the longevity of an SSD, of course. This includes heat, not having adequate cooling within the system, writing too much data to the drive (most SSDs will have endurance ratings), and unexpected power losses. An uninterruptible power supply (UPS) should almost always be present and connected if you're running NAS or building out a home lab. These can trigger hardware failures that could result in data loss.

Adding an SSD is the best upgrade for an old PC to improve its performance

Speed up your aging PC by adding a new SSD.

By  Karthik Iyer

Why buy HDDs over SSDs?

The primary reason for me and many others to continue purchasing hard drives is cost. These units are notably cheaper than SSD counterparts, with some models coming in at $0.02 per GB of storage space — and that's for server (or NAS) grade hardware too. Then there's capacity. SSDs have grown in size over the years, but you'll still need to spend the big bucks and pick up enterprise-grade storage to store any more than 8 TB of data per drive.

That's simply not the case with hard drives, which can be found in capacities ranging from just 1 TB to 24 TB for NAS. Picking up a 16 TB drive allows me to configure a RAID for local data protection without using all available drive bays. Going with larger drives also means the storage pool can be considerably larger when all bays are populated with drives, compared to using an array of 8 TB SSDs. There's also the case that you don't typically need SSD speeds.

A NAS or home server will have up to two 1Gb or 2.5Gb ports, which can be easily saturated with heavy file transfer requests and a mechanical drive. These bulky spinners may seem slow with just 550 MB/s compared to the fastest PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSDs, but that's more than enough for a 100 MB/s LAN connection. Even moving up to 10 Gb may not show much benefit moving from HDDs to SSDs, but this depends entirely on your LAN and how you use data.

For me, it makes more sense to stay with mechanical drives for now. And replacing drives in TrueNAS, which just so happens to be my primary storage NAS OS, is straightforward enough that larger HDDs can be rotated in without causing much fuss. That said, even if you're running premium drives and have a RAID configured, you should always consider following the 3-2-1 backup strategy to ensure you're adequately protected against data loss. Drives can fail ... even SSDs.

Deals

Storage Deals: Big Savings on HDDs, NAS & Networking

Explore deals on storage and networking gear—discounts on high-capacity HDDs, SSDs, NAS enclosures, switches, and UPS units. Save on upgrades for home servers and NAS builds, accessories and networking essentials to boost capacity and resilience.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

Noise and heat could be a factor in option against HDDs, especially when using multiple units inside devices near where you work. That's not the case for me since all my equipment is in racks inside the garage. It's handy since we don't need that room to be quiet, and there's ample airflow to aid with keeping all the systems cool.

I'll make the switch ... eventually

SSDs are the future, but there's also a case for HDDs. While technology continues to advance, we'll likely be at a point where more SSD capacities will be readily available for consumers, but we're not quite there yet. I'll happily make the switch to SSDs once capacities increase, endurance improves, and costs continue to fall. I can't justify more than $300 for an 8TB SSD when that same budget could fund multiple HDDs of the same size.
